comments.author: MaraDönerFan42
comments.text: Michiii in a Porsche at the Nordschleife. This is what we've been waiting for. His wet-weather car control in Melbourne, the sprint podium in China — put that talent in a GT3 car on the most demanding circuit in the world and you get pure magic. And if Väisänen and Thompson join? That's not a privateer entry, that's a fantasy team. The kid who grew up karting in Greece is about to share a car with the championship leader. This sport is beautiful.

replies.author: GridGazer
replies.text: The championship leader sharing a car with a rookie and an F2 driver at the Nürburgring is the kind of crossover that either becomes legendary or ends with a very awkward phone call to Maranello. I'm not sure which, but I'm here for it.

replies.author: F2Scout
replies.text: Thompson has already won twice in GT3 this year, and now he might get a Nürburgring 24 drive with his mentor Väisänen. That Mercedes junior programme is giving him an incredible breadth of experience — even if he's doing half of it in an Aston Martin.

replies.author: RoadCourseRealist
replies.text: Thompson has two wins — on an oval. The only oval in the series. Every other round he's finished 25th or worse, crashed, or been invisible. Mosport: 34th and DNF. Goiânia: 32nd and 36th. If he's the third driver in this Porsche, they'd better hope the Nürburgring suddenly becomes a banked oval. The Nordschleife has 170 corners and none of them forgive a driver who can't keep it together on a road course.

replies.author: F2Scout
replies.text: Thompson's road course struggles are real, but you're ignoring that his Indianapolis finish — 18th and 19th on a road course — was a step forward. He's a project driver who is learning in real time. The Nürburgring would be exactly the kind of baptism that forces growth. And having Väisänen as a mentor sharing the car? That's the best classroom he could ask for.

replies.author: MercEngineer
replies.text: Thompson's development is not linear. The GT3 programme was designed to expose him to different machinery and race conditions, precisely to iron out the inconsistencies. A Nürburgring drive — if it happens — is part of that education, not a reward for being perfect. No one at Mercedes expects him to be the finished article yet.

replies.author: ElladaRacing
replies.text: Ο Μιχίλης είναι ο καλύτερος! Πρώτος στην Μελβούρνη, τώρα στο Nürburgring! Η Ελλάδα έχει άσο! Θα πάρω αεροπλάνο να τον δω! Μπράβο παλικάρι μου! 🇬🇷🏁

replies.author: MaraDönerFan42
replies.text: I only understood 'Michiii', 'Melbourne', and 'Nürburgring', but the energy is absolutely correct. Welcome to the club.

comments.author: GridGazer
comments.text: Let's be clear: the championship leader, with two wins from two races, is rumoured to be entering the Nürburgring 24 Hours in a privateer Porsche during his summer break. If this happens, it's either the coolest thing a title favourite has done in years or a risk management nightmare. Probably both. The WEC paddock sighting with MaraDöner suggests this isn't idle gossip — there's substance here.

replies.author: TifosiForever
replies.text: Väisänen is a racer's racer. He's leading the championship because he understands cars, not in spite of it. If he wants to spend his break learning the Nordschleife in a GT car, that's his call. Ferrari wouldn't allow it if they thought it compromised anything. And honestly? It's exactly the kind of thing that makes him the driver he is.

replies.author: RealityCheck
replies.text: The risk is real, though. A single accident at the Nordschleife can end a season — or worse. Hülkenberg did it and won Le Mans the same year, but he wasn't leading the F1 championship. Ferrari's silence on this is notable. They might be waiting to see how serious it is before commenting.

replies.author: PaddockInsider
replies.text: The Qatar paddock meeting is the smoking gun. Väisänen and MaraDöner don't have an obvious connection — they're not former teammates, they're not from the same junior programme. The only thread is Thompson, and the article confirms Väisänen has been mentoring him. That's how these things start: a shared contact, a conversation, then a group chat, then a livery reveal.

replies.author: AstonishinglyMediocre
replies.text: Väisänen mentoring Thompson is a nice story, but Thompson has been a magnet for trouble in GT3. He binned it at Sebring, finished 40th at Long Beach, and has more DNFs than top-20 finishes. If I'm Väisänen, I'm not sure I want the guy who can't keep it out of the wall at Mosport sharing my Nürburgring car at 3am in the fog. That's not mentorship — that's a liability.

replies.author: F2Scout
replies.text: Thompson's oval wins weren't flukes — he led every lap of both races at Auto Club. That takes genuine car control. The road course results are inconsistent, yes, but the Nürburgring is its own beast. Some drivers who struggle on conventional road courses find the Nordschleife's rhythm more intuitive. And again: Väisänen is mentoring him. The Finn wouldn't waste his time on a lost cause.

replies.author: GreekLightning
replies.text: MaraDöner is not just a rookie — he's the future of this sport. You see Melbourne? You see China? That's raw talent. And now he's going to the Nürburgring because he wants to race everything. That's the spirit of a true champion. I don't care if Väisänen is there too; Michiii will steal the show. He always does. 🇬🇷💪

replies.author: MaraDönerFan42
replies.text: Exactly. The pace he showed in that Alpine in the wet was not normal. Give him a Porsche on the Nordschleife and he'll find seconds where others find armco. The fact he's only 18 and already seeking out the hardest race in the world tells you everything.

comments.author: ChaosReigns
comments.text: An 18-year-old rookie podium-finisher, the championship leader, and a Mercedes junior who's already won two GT3 races — all potentially sharing a Porsche at the Nürburgring. This is the crossover event of the season and it's happening during a F1 break. The 2026 season refuses to be normal and I am deeply grateful.

replies.author: VoiceOfTheFan
replies.text: THE CHAMPIONSHIP LEADER AT THE NORDSCHLEIFE!! I DON'T CARE IF IT'S RISKY THIS IS WHY I LOVE MOTORSPORT. THESE GUYS JUST WANT TO RACE ANYTHING ANYWHERE. IMAGINE THE ONBOARD.

replies.author: RoadCourseRealist
replies.text: Thompson's 'two GT3 wins' are doing a lot of heavy lifting. They both came on an oval — the only oval in the series. On road courses, his best result is 18th. The Nürburgring is not an oval. If he's in the car for the night stint, the Porsche had better budget for bodywork repairs.

replies.author: ChaosReigns
replies.text: Counterpoint: the kid's 19 and already driving F2, GT3, and maybe the Nürburgring 24 in the same season. That's not a failure — that's a resume most drivers would kill for. The inconsistency is part of the learning curve. Let him cook.

comments.author: UnpopularOpinion
comments.text: I'll say it: F1 drivers doing endurance races in their breaks is an unnecessary risk. The Nürburgring is the most dangerous circuit in the world. One mistake in the fog at 3am and your championship campaign is over. There's a reason most teams forbid this. Alpine allowing MaraDöner is one thing — he's a rookie with nothing to lose. If Ferrari let Väisänen do this while leading the title fight, they're insane.

replies.author: MaraDönerFan42
replies.text: These are racing drivers, not porcelain dolls. The Nordschleife taught generations of drivers car control that made them better in F1. MaraDöner racing there will come back sharper. Väisänen doing it would prove he's not just fast — he's fearless. That's what champions are made of.

replies.author: SbinnalaEnjoyer
replies.text: Drivers have been doing this for decades. The ones who wrap themselves in cotton wool don't become legends. The ones who race everything with wheels do. Let them cook.

comments.author: TifosiForever
comments.text: If Väisänen is really doing this, it tells you everything about his mindset. The championship leader isn't resting on a 50-point cushion — he's seeking out the hardest race in the world to make himself better. That's not arrogance. That's a champion who refuses to stop learning. Forza Ferrari.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: Or it's a distraction. Kevu will be in the simulator, not risking his season on a foggy German mountain. There's more than one way to prepare for a title fight. The Nordschleife doesn't teach you how to manage a 24-race F1 campaign.

comments.author: FaktLap1Legend
comments.text: A Porsche? Cute. Sam Fakt would win the Nürburgring 24 Hours solo, on foot, while meditating. The GOAT doesn't need a co-driver — he needs only the open road and the sound of his own greatness. 🐐

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: He can't finish a single lap of a Grand Prix. The Nordschleife has 170 corners. He'd be out before the first carousel. Please stop.

comments.author: ConstructorsMathematician
comments.text: Constructors' perspective: if Väisänen gets injured at the Nürburgring, Ferrari lose 57 points of their constructors' total instantly. Cachinho has zero. That would drop them from first to potentially fourth or fifth depending on the weekend's results. The risk-reward ratio here is catastrophic. One privateer GT entry could destroy a championship built over months. The fact Ferrari haven't denied it suggests they're either confident it won't happen or they're still negotiating.

replies.author: TifosiForever
replies.text: Or they trust their driver to make good decisions. Väisänen hasn't put a wheel wrong all season. If he thinks he can do it safely, I trust him more than a spreadsheet.

comments.author: TheRealThompsonSkeptic
comments.text: Everyone hyping this 'mentorship' angle is ignoring the obvious: Alex Thompson is a liability on road courses. The guy has one top-20 finish outside of an oval. One. He binned it at Sebring, spun at Long Beach, and crashed at Mosport. Now he's going to share a car with the championship leader at the most unforgiving circuit in the world? If Väisänen wants a teammate who won't put it in the barriers at 4am, he should look elsewhere. The Mercedes junior programme may have faith, but the results say 'stay away from the Nordschleife.'

replies.author: F2Scout
replies.text: Thompson's 2026 road course record is poor, no argument. But the Nürburgring is a different discipline entirely — it's more about rhythm and car control than standard road course precision. And he wouldn't be the first driver to find the Nordschleife suits him better than a traditional circuit. The kid has talent; the question is whether he can translate it. The only way to find out is to let him race.

replies.author: NeutralObserver_99
replies.text: As someone with no particular allegiance here, the criticism of Thompson is fair based on his road course results, but the Nürburgring is a completely different challenge. It's not a typical road course — it's a marathon through a forest. Some drivers who struggle on modern tracks find the old-school Nordschleife more to their liking. And having a mentor like Väisänen in the same car is an enormous safety net.
